g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of algorithms. This fundamental concept forms the backbone of all computational processes in computer science.
g.text: university - Analysis and Design of Algorithms

g.data-narration: An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined instructions that transforms an input into a desired output, forming the core of problem-solving in computer science.
The term algorithm originates from Persian mathematician al-Khwarizmi, whose 9th-century algebraic works influenced modern computational thinking.
g.text: His work on algebra → modern computing

g.data-narration: Every valid algorithm must possess five essential properties: finiteness, definiteness, input, output, and effectiveness. These ensure algorithmic validity.
g.text: Five Essential Properties of an Algorithm

g.data-narration: Finiteness requires algorithms to terminate after finite steps. Definiteness ensures each instruction is unambiguous and precisely defined.
Input provides data to process (zero or more inputs allowed), while output produces meaningful results (one or more) related to the input.
Effectiveness ensures each operation is basic enough to be performed by a person using only pencil and paper, making algorithms practical.
g.text: Each step must be basic enough to be carried out, in principle, by a person using
only pencil and paper. Operations should be

g.data-narration: Algorithms can be represented through pseudocode for logic, flowcharts for visual flow, and programming code for actual execution.
Time complexity measures execution time as a function of input size, typically expressed in Big-O notation to classify algorithm performance.
g.text: Measure of execution time as a function of input size (n).

g.data-narration: Space complexity measures memory required to execute an algorithm as a function of input size, also expressed in Big-O notation.
g.text: Amount of memory required to execute an algorithm as a function of input size.
(e.g., O(1) for constant space, O(n) for linear space)

g.data-narration: Visual comparison shows how different time complexities grow with input size, from constant O(1) to exponential O(2^n).
g.text: O(1) - Constant: Fixed time regardless of input
O(log n) - Logarithmic: Divides problem each step
O(n) - Linear: Processes each element once
O(n²) - Quadratic: Nested iterations over input

g.data-narration: Finding the maximum element in an array demonstrates O(n) time complexity with O(1) space, iterating through each element once.
g.text: if A[i] > max then max ← A[i]

g.data-narration: Checking if a number is prime uses O(√n) time complexity by only testing divisors up to the square root of n.
g.text: if n mod i = 0 then return false

g.data-narration: Linear search demonstrates variable time complexity: O(1) best case, O(n) worst case, checking each element sequentially.
g.text: if A[i] = target then return i
Best Case: O(1) - element at first position
Worst Case: O(n) - element at last or not present
Average Case: O(n) - scans half array on average

g.data-narration: In summary, algorithms are finite sequences of instructions with five essential properties, analyzed by time and space complexity.
g.text: • Algorithm: Finite sequence of well-defined instructions
• Five Properties: Finiteness, Definiteness, Input, Output, Effectiveness
• Analysis: Time complexity (execution time) and Space complexity (memory)
• Big-O: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ)

g.data-narration: For exams, memorize the five properties, practice pseudocode writing, and understand how to analyze algorithm complexity.
g.text: 1. Memorize five algorithm properties (frequently tested in university exams)
2. Know difference between time and space complexity
3. Practice writing clear pseudocode with proper indentation
4. Remember: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ)
8. Focus on understanding WHY not just HOW
